There's an interesting functional programming language experiment I found on Twitter but can't seem to find now. It adds efficiency concerns into the type system so you can explicitly reason about performance, like memory and time details. I think it's somewhat similar to Rust in its linear types that specify the number of times something can be accessed but I don't know almost anything about Rust so I may be mistaken

the erlang vm (BEAM) allows you to spawn a lightweight process with a limit size heap, if the process tries to allocate more it crashes, you can also start a timer from the spawning process and kill it via its reference when it goes over your limit

regarding detecting infinite loops and "seing resource usage" before execution, it sounds like the halting problem :slightly_smiling_face:

you can guess/detect the basic ones, but all of them I think it's not possible

note: crashing in erlang is not a bad thing, also process is more like people know as green threads

Do you agree with the following principle? 
"A programming environment should prevent users from executing programs that accidentally use up all their systems' resources."

Do you agree with the following principle? 
"A programming environment should prevent users from executing programs that accidentally use up all their systems' resources."

Maybe DTrace could help? (<http://dtrace.org/blogs/about/>) I've never used it directly, but it's the infrastructure that Xcode and Instruments build on to do all kinds of analysis during runtime. That’s not exactly what you asked for, but the CPU and memory gauges that Xcode displays when you run an app, that's exactly to get a feeling for potential memory leaks or see when a single thread maxes out the CPU for too long. As far as I know that's all based on DTrace and custom probes and there are some ancient mystics who I know can do magical things with it. Sadly, I’m not one of them.

<@UAL7940NM> how often does that happen? also, if not on the language runtime you can enforce process limits at the OS level, with cgroups in linux for example
<@UAL7940NM> how often does that happen? also, if not on the language runtime you can enforce process limits at the OS level, with cgroups in linux for example

```# Allows only 1ms every 100ms to simulate a slow system
cgset -r cpu.cfs_period_us=100000 -r cpu.cfs_quota_us=1000 sandbox
 
# Limit usage at 10% for a multi core system
cgset -r cpu.cfs_period_us=100000 \
     -r cpu.cfs_quota_us=$[ 10000 * $(getconf _NPROCESSORS_ONLN) ] \
           sandbox`

# Set a limit of 2Gb
cgset -r memory.limit_in_bytes=2G sandbox
 
# Get memory stats used by the cgroup
cgget -r memory.stat sandbox```

```# Allows only 1ms every 100ms to simulate a slow system

           sandbox`

# Set a limit of 2Gb
cgset -r memory.limit_in_bytes=2G sandbox
 
# Get memory stats used by the cgroup
cgget -r memory.stat sandbox```

Generally love Fogus’ blogging and this is no exception. This stuck out to me:

> Any work in IPEs is likely to require a huge amount of effort and brain-power to crack. Indeed, you could expect to spend the next 10-years just gaining a deep understanding of the problem space and contributing to the rich landscape…It’s difficult to start a project that most likely will merely provide footnotes to the next generation of proposed solutions.

> Do you agree with the following principle? 
"A programming environment should prevent users from executing programs that accidentally use up all their systems' resources."

Yes I agree with that on the grounds that it is part of the more general principle that *the computer should communicate to you the potentially undesirable, unforeseen consequences of what you’re asking of it* (<https://twitter.com/stevekrouse/status/1083891157858426886?s=09>)

Also I found it: 
<https://granule-project.github.io/>

Maybe the biggest difference between programming and just using computer is that while programming you are making all the decisions beforehand. Uncatched exceptions are just situations where programmer has not made decision what to do. Distinction between user and programmer could be much smaller. When unmade decission is met simply give control back to user (instead of crashing).

What if WriteFileException would always open normal save dialog without extra effort so that user can save for somewhere else if default location was unavailable and same if reading fails.

Can you give us a big picture description of the type of projects inside Intentional <@UHPAERALD>

The main project while I was there was a general-purpose structured editor called the Intentional Domain Workbench. You defined a schema for your language, we gave you an IDE, which you could progressively enhance with custom notations and functionality.
_Everything_ in the system was built around tree transformations - the entire editor was a series of compiler passes from domain code to an editor language to a layout language to a graphics language - and all transformations had rich mappings between their input and output, so you could connect a pixel on the screen back to its source. 
I spent most of my time working on persistence and version control - merging of trees based on full edit history, unobtrusively modelling conflicts, etc. 
We had a couple of pilot customers that we built workbenches for, to prove out the approach - iirc we demoed one based around administrating pensions a few times. There are screenshots for sure, not sure if there's video.
It sounds similar to Jetbrains MPS. If you are familiar with it, can you explain how it's the same or different?

Version control (and merging and comparing) of trees (versus pain text) would be invaluable to what many of us are trying to do. I'd be interested in learning more about that. Is that something that might ever become available? (As a separate tool, or as part of IDW?)

I definitely have in the past. What’s helped is taking regular breaks (once every 60 - 90 minutes) and structuring my work around the breaks. For me, sitting (or even standing) for more than 60 minutes starts to cause discomfort and misalignment in my posture (if you want to go deep on this topic, I recommend this book <https://www.amazon.com/New-Rules-Posture-Stand-Modern/dp/1594771243/ref=sr_1_3?keywords=new+posture&qid=1554459586&s=gateway&sr=8-3>).

I’ve also found walking around for a few minutes, getting water, and getting out of the headspace of the problem quite restorative. Sometimes, I also get some creative ideas (presumably b/c I’m able to use my full brain when walking vs when I’m sitting)!

This is all anecdotal FWIW!

Yes, MPS is the closest thing I'm aware of that's actually available. Unfortunately I haven't spent much time actually using it, so I can't really comment on specific differences except very superficially, ie. Intentional's tooling was based on C#/.NET instead of Java. 
The one time I tried it was many years ago, iirc the only thing I tried was to rename a thing and see if the references updated, which iirc they didn't. I don't know if I misunderstood something, am misremembering, or if they've fixed that since, but such an edit would have impossible with Intentional's representation; names were a property of the thing, not references to the thing. This was core; you could have any number of names assigned to something - i18n for your variables, both short and long names, etc. At one point we added "friendly" names to some of our core language features, which were originally named with Hungarian notation :)

Intentional's product strategy is something I can't speculate on; they started staffing up significantly around the time I left, but I honestly don't know what all those developers were doing. I know they were purchased by Microsoft a couple of years ago now; whether that means we'll see the public release of development tools or some other application of the tech, I don't know.

Another question, if this is something experienced, does it have it’s upsides? For example if you could never experience it again would that be worthwhile? Or does that state come with some enhanced coding ability?

Another question, if this is something experienced, does it have it’s upsides? For example if you could never experience it again would that be worthwhile? Or does that state come with some enhanced coding ability?

At a very high level, every edit in the tool appended to a changelog that described the edits in enough detail that it could be played back to reproduce the document, or undone. Every node in the source tree had a unique identity, so we could directly tell the difference between "delete/insert" and "move". If we couldn't apply a change cleanly, we'd insert information about the conflict into the document. (The "conflict" domain was simply another language that could be freely intermixed into any document due to the flexibility of our tree representation.) Charles patented this as "Perfect Source Control".

I never thought about it, but now that you describe it, I definitely “suffer” from “developer head” — it’s like you try to switch to another front most app in your head, but there are so many processes from the programming task still burning most of your CPU cycles.

Because I haven’t really thought about it much, I can’t say how I usually get out of it. But now that I’m aware I’ll watch this closely.

Sleeping definitely helps, but I guess that’s cheating because it’s like turning it off and back on again. :joy:

Well, i am not familiar with the "developer head" state, but am very familiar with what i call "Artichoke Mode", where after a certain point you tire and become as stupid as an artichoke, and enter the zone of negative productivity, where you remove good work, and introduce errors. Learning to stop before entering "Artichoke Mode" is part of learning to be a professional programmer.

I find the whole conversation about changelogs, and fancy source control to be depressing. If Tolstoy who wrote War & Peace, one of the greatest books ever written entirely in longhand, can create a complex, threaded story, without worrying about backtracking, why is it that people are so interested in what the code used to be? That isn't productivity, source control is about damage control from people breaking stuff who can't understand the implications of the changes they are making, because stuff is too tightly interconnected. In my system i am striving for super-compartmentalization, so that changes don't ripple throughout the system, and have unseen, unpredictable consequences. CSS is my poster child for unpredictable results from small changes. I think CSS is the most devilish thing ever devised in computers, one of the primary motivations for my development efforts is my extreme distaste for CSS.

Anyway, if you can author sequentially, and the computer turns it into recursion, and depth-first/breadth-first searches, and automatic sorting, with no joins or tricky concepts, it should be possible for individuals to write large products with no stress.

I always found that programs with no branches and a straight down flow were easy to program. So the goal is to have zero IF statements, and linear flow. Let the computer do the tricky bit of  re-sequencing. That is the goal.

<@UEQ6M68H0> In practice for real world projects history of the code is crucially important. If I release a new version of my product and a customer experiences a bug that didn’t exist in the previous version, the first thing I’d go for is bisecting on when was it introduced and what change made it appear. One may argue that by definition there are no bugs in War & Peace,, only features. Well maybe War is a bug :wink:
